artifacts.summary: Official-source discovery found Concordia CTL's central GenAI teaching guidelines, CTL assessment-design GenAI page, Academic & Behavioural Conduct academic integrity page, Student Success GenAI writing suggestions, and CTL educational technology privacy guidelines. Claims are scoped to these official sources; library training material, a department page, a continuing-education course page, and a research-showcase item were rejected or not used as university-wide policy evidence.

artifacts.claimText: Concordia's CTL GenAI FAQ says the university does not currently have an official policy regarding the use of GenAI, and explains that GenAI has not officially been adopted by the university so its use cannot be required in coursework.

citation.citationTitle: Guidelines for Teaching with Generative Artificial Intelligence
citation.publisher: Concordia University Centre for Teaching and Learning

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidelines for Teaching with Generative Artificial Intelligence
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The university does not currently have an official policy regarding the use of GenAI. One reason is that GenAI has not officially been adopted by the university, meaning that its use cannot be required in coursework.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: FAQ for Faculty > Is there an official university policy about AI?

citation.citationTitle: Guidelines for Teaching with Generative Artificial Intelligence
citation.publisher: Concordia University Centre for Teaching and Learning

artifacts.notes: Approved as a narrow source-status claim based on CTL FAQ language; not promoted as canonical or human reviewed.

artifacts.claimText: Concordia CTL encourages faculty to clearly state their position on GenAI use in courses, including in the syllabus and in class, and provides adaptable sample statements for encouraged, limited, or prohibited use.

citation.citationTitle: Guidelines for Teaching with Generative Artificial Intelligence
citation.publisher: Concordia University Centre for Teaching and Learning

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidelines for Teaching with Generative Artificial Intelligence
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: All faculty are encouraged to clearly state their position on the use of GenAI in their courses. This should be stated clearly in the syllabus and should be discussed in class to ensure shared understanding.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Setting and communicating course policy regarding the use of GenAI

citation.citationTitle: Guidelines for Teaching with Generative Artificial Intelligence
citation.publisher: Concordia University Centre for Teaching and Learning

artifacts.notes: Approved as guidance for faculty communication, not a binding university-wide GenAI permission rule.

citation.citationTitle: Navigate assessment design and generative AI
citation.publisher: Concordia University Centre for Teaching and Learning

artifacts.sourceTitle: Navigate assessment design and generative AI
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: At Concordia, all staff and students have access to Copilot Chat through an institutional Microsoft 365 license. Copilot Chat is available on the Edge browser or through the link copilot.cloud.microsoft. It requires authentication (using Concordia credentials) and it offers Enterprise Data Protection.

citation.publisher: Concordia University Centre for Teaching and Learning

artifacts.claimText: Concordia Student Success guidance tells students not to assume GenAI is allowed if an instructor does not mention it, and says permitted use of generative tools for academic work must be cited.

citation.citationTitle: Suggestions for using Generative AI in academic writing

artifacts.sourceTitle: Suggestions for using Generative AI in academic writing
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Do not assume it is allowed if your professor does not mention it. Always read instructions carefully and ask your professor if you need clarification. If you are permitted to use these types of generative tools for your academic work, you must cite them.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Follow your professor's instructions; Permission and attribution

citation.citationTitle: Suggestions for using Generative AI in academic writing

artifacts.notes: Approved as student-facing guidance, not as a formal Academic Code amendment.

artifacts.claimText: Concordia's educational technology guidelines say a Privacy Impact Assessment is required whenever third-party technology uses, shares, or stores personal information, and that a written agreement with Concordia is required after a successful PIA.

citation.publisher: Concordia University Centre for Teaching and Learning

artifacts.sourceTitle: Concordia Educational Technology Guidelines for Faculty and Students
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: A Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) must be conducted whenever a third-party technology uses, shares, or stores Personal Information. The third party must also sign an agreement with Concordia University following the successful completion of a PIA.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Concordia Educational Technology Guidelines for Faculty and Students

citation.citationTitle: Concordia Educational Technology Guidelines for Faculty and Students
citation.publisher: Concordia University Centre for Teaching and Learning

artifacts.notes: Approved as a general educational-technology privacy claim cited by the GenAI pages; not specific to one AI vendor.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Academic integrity

artifacts.notes: Approved as background academic-integrity source status; it does not assert that the Academic Code specifically names GenAI.

artifacts.title: Concordia University GenAI Policy Source Review
artifacts.summary: Official Concordia sources located during this crawl indicate that CTL states there is no current official university policy regarding GenAI use. Concordia provides CTL guidance for faculty to communicate course-level GenAI expectations, prohibits staff/faculty use of unapproved online AI detectors due to privacy laws, identifies Copilot Chat access with Enterprise Data Protection, advises students to follow instructor permission and cite permitted GenAI use, and requires PIA review and agreement for third-party educational technologies handling personal information.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
This staged report does not treat CTL sample syllabus statements, department pages, library learning modules, course listings, or research repository records as binding university-wide GenAI policy.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
